column9: Use this as a first-pass regional morphology anchor rather than treating all vertebral bodies as having the same expected size.
column15: How does vertebral body size normally change from cervical spine to coccyx?
column16: Vertebral bodies generally increase in size from the cervical spine to the lumbar spine, then decrease from the sacrum to the coccyx.
column21:
🧭 Vertebral Body and Ligaments
RadPrimer normal anatomy overview of vertebral body morphology across spinal regions, sacral and coccygeal segment fusion, major spinal ligaments, and the normal lumbosacral transitional body variant.

🔶 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • ALL = anterior longitudinal ligament.
  • PLL = posterior longitudinal ligament.
🧠 Regional Vertebral Body Map
  • Vertebral bodies generally increase in size from cervical to lumbar spine, then decrease from sacrum to coccyx.
  • C1 atlas has no vertebral body or spinous process and is formed by anterior and posterior arches, lateral masses, and transverse processes.
  • C2 axis has a vertebral body with the dens/odontoid process; its transverse processes contain L-shaped foramina for the vertebral arteries.
  • C3-C6 share small thin bodies, transverse diameter greater than anteroposterior diameter, triangular central canals, uncinate processes, and transverse foramina for the vertebral arteries.
  • C7 is marked by the longest spinous process.
  • Thoracic vertebrae have heart-shaped bodies, round central canals, caudodorsally directed spinous processes, and costal articular facets for the ribs.
  • Lumbar vertebrae have large, wide, thick bodies with strong pedicles and laminae.
  • The sacrum is a triangular fusion of 5 segments; the coccyx is a fusion of 3-5 segments.
📍 Ligament Map
  • ALL runs along the ventral spine from skull to sacrum and is firmly attached at vertebral body ends but loosely attached at the mid-disc.
  • PLL runs along the dorsal vertebral bodies from skull to sacrum, attaching at discs and vertebral body margins.
  • Ligamentum flavum is the largest elastic ligament and connects adjacent laminae from C2 to the lumbosacral junction.
  • Intertransverse ligaments run between transverse processes; interspinous ligaments run between adjacent spinous processes; supraspinous ligaments run along spinous process tips from C7 to sacrum.
  • Ligamentum nuchae extends from the external occipital protuberance to C7 and merges with the supraspinous ligament in the cervical spine.
⚠️ Variant / Pitfall
  • Transitional lumbosacral bodies occur in up to 25% of normal patients.
  • Sacralization ranges from expanded L5 transverse processes articulating with the sacrum to incorporation of L5 into the sacrum.
  • Lumbarization describes elevation of S1 above the sacral fusion mass with a lumbar vertebral body shape.
  • Sacralization and lumbarization may look similar locally; correct level nomenclature requires evaluation of the entire spinal axis.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Use regional morphology to orient the vertebral level, use ligament course and attachment to map the spinal support structures, and always treat lumbosacral transitional anatomy as a normal variant that can create level-naming errors unless the entire spinal axis is evaluated.

column9: At C1, absence of a body and spinous process is expected anatomy, not a missing-structure abnormality.
column15: What normal osseous pattern defines the C1 atlas?
column16: C1 has no vertebral body and no spinous process. It has a circular shape formed by anterior and posterior arches, 2 lateral masses, and transverse processes.

column9: Use the dens as the primary C2 orientation anchor, then confirm the broad posterior elements and vertebral artery foramina.
column15: What normal osseous features define the C2 axis?
column16: C2 has a vertebral body with the dens/odontoid process, large flat ovoid articular facets, broad pedicles, thick laminae, and transverse processes containing L-shaped foramina for the vertebral arteries.

column9: This is an intentional compact framework card for subaxial cervical morphology; the transverse foramina and triangular canal are the most useful regional anchors.
column15: Which morphology anchors normal C3-C6 vertebrae?
column16: C3-C6 have small thin bodies relative to the arch, transverse diameter greater than anteroposterior diameter, triangular central canals, superior surface uncinate processes, short posterolaterally directed pedicles, rhomboid lateral masses, transverse foramina for the vertebral arteries, and usually short bifid spinous processes at C3-C5.

column9: Costal facets are the most specific regional anchor because they link thoracic vertebrae to rib articulation.
column15: Which morphology anchors a thoracic vertebra?
column16: Thoracic vertebrae have heart-shaped bodies, round central canals, short posteriorly directed pedicles, broad thick laminae, caudodorsally directed spinous processes, vertical flat superior articular processes facing posteriorly, and costal articular facets on the body and transverse processes for rib articulation.

column15: What normal feature makes T12 resemble the upper lumbar spine?
column16: T12 resembles upper lumbar vertebrae because its inferior facet is directed more laterally.

column9: Use the large body and robust posterior elements as the first lumbar anchors, then use facet orientation for confirmation.
column15: Which morphology anchors a lumbar vertebra?
column16: Lumbar vertebrae have large, wide, thick bodies, strong thick posteriorly directed pedicles, strong broad laminae, superior articular processes facing dorsomedially, and inferior articular processes facing anterolaterally.

column9: Treat the sacrum as a fused segmental bone rather than as separate mobile vertebrae.
column15: What is the core normal structural map of the sacrum?
column16: The sacrum is a large triangular bone formed by fusion of 5 segments. It has a base, apex, pelvic surface, dorsal surface, lateral surfaces, and 2 alae. The base is round or ovoid and articulates with L5; the apex is the inferior aspect of S5 and articulates with the coccyx.

column15: What normal landmarks define the pelvic surface of the sacrum?
column16: The pelvic surface is concave, crossed by 4 transverse ridges, and has anterior sacral foramina at the lateral ends of the ridges.

column9: This is an ordered posterior sacral map; preserve the medial-to-lateral sequence when reviewing the dorsal sacrum.
column15: From medial to lateral, what landmarks organize the dorsal surface of the sacrum?
column16: Medial to lateral: median sacral crest in the midline, sacral groove on each side of the crest, intermediate crest lateral to the groove, posterior sacral foramina lateral to the intermediate crest, and lateral crest lateral to the sacral foramina.

column15: What normal sacral anatomy articulates with the iliac bone?
column16: The lateral sacral surfaces are formed by costal and transverse processes, and the alae on the sides articulate with the iliac bone.

column9: A bifid coccygeal apex may be normal when it matches the described coccygeal morphology.
column15: What is the normal structural map of the coccyx?
column16: The coccyx is a fusion of 3-5 segments. Its anterior surface is concave with transverse ridges, its posterior surface is convex with transverse ridges, and its apex is round, directed caudally, and may be bifid.

column9: Use the ventral vertebral body surface as the ALL anchor, then remember that its fiber layers span progressively more levels from deep to superficial.
column15: What is the course and attachment pattern of the anterior longitudinal ligament?
column16: The ALL is a fibrous band on the ventral surface of the spine from skull to sacrum. It is firmly attached at the ends of each vertebral body and loosely attached at the midsection of the disc. Its deep fibers span 1 disc, intermediate fibers span 2-3 discs, and superficial fibers span 4-5 levels.

column9: Use the dorsal vertebral body surface as the PLL anchor; the lumbar PLL changes caliber across body versus disc levels.
column15: What is the course and regional shape pattern of the posterior longitudinal ligament?
column16: The PLL runs on the dorsal surface of vertebral bodies from skull to sacrum and attaches at discs and vertebral body margins. It is broad and uniform in the cervical and thoracic spine, but in the lumbar spine it is narrow at the vertebral body and broad at the disc level.

column9: Localize ligamentum flavum by the lamina-to-lamina interval rather than by the vertebral body margin.
column15: What is the normal course and regional morphology of the ligamentum flavum?
column16: The ligamentum flavum is the largest elastic ligament in the body. It connects adjacent laminae from C2 to the lumbosacral junction, extends from the apophyseal joint capsule to the junction of the lamina with the spinous process, is thin and broad in the cervical region, and is thicker in the lumbar region.

column9: This is a layer-style posterior ligament map: ligamentum flavum deep at the laminae, interspinous between spinous processes, supraspinous along spinous process tips.
column15: How should the interspinous and supraspinous ligaments be placed relative to the ligamentum flavum?
column16: The interspinous ligaments connect adjoining spinous processes and lie between the ligamentum flavum and supraspinous ligaments. The supraspinous ligaments extend from the tips of spinous processes from C7 to sacrum and fuse with the dorsal margin of the interspinous ligament.

column15: What regional strength pattern is described for the intertransverse and interspinous ligaments?
column16: Intertransverse ligaments extend between transverse processes; they are sparse or absent in the cervical spine and stronger in the thoracic spine where associated with muscles. Interspinous ligaments connect adjoining spinous processes and are strongest in the lumbar spine.

column15: What is the normal relationship between the supraspinous ligament and ligamentum nuchae?
column16: The supraspinous ligament extends from C7 to the sacrum and merges with the ligamentum nuchae in the cervical spine. The ligamentum nuchae extends from the external occipital protuberance to C7.

column9: These are normal variants that can alter apparent lumbosacral segmentation.
column15: What normal lumbosacral transitional body variants are described?
column16: Transitional lumbosacral bodies occur in up to 25% of normal patients. Sacralization of a lumbar body ranges from expanded L5 transverse processes articulating with the top of the sacrum to incorporation of L5 into the sacrum. Lumbarization of the sacrum describes elevation of S1 above the sacral fusion mass with an assumed lumbar body shape.

  • Thoracic vertebrae have heart-shaped bodies, round central canals, caudodorsally directed spinous processes, and costal articular facets for the ribs.
  • Lumbar vertebrae have large, wide, thick bodies with strong pedicles and laminae.
  • The sacrum is a triangular fusion of 5 segments; the coccyx is a fusion of 3-5 segments.
📍 Ligament Map
  • ALL runs along the ventral spine from skull to sacrum and is firmly attached at vertebral body ends but loosely attached at the mid-disc.
  • PLL runs along the dorsal vertebral bodies from skull to sacrum, attaching at discs and vertebral body margins.
  • Ligamentum flavum is the largest elastic ligament and connects adjacent laminae from C2 to the lumbosacral junction.
  • Intertransverse ligaments run between transverse processes; interspinous ligaments run between adjacent spinous processes; supraspinous ligaments run along spinous process tips from C7 to sacrum.
  • Ligamentum nuchae extends from the external occipital protuberance to C7 and merges with the supraspinous ligament in the cervical spine.
⚠️ Variant / Pitfall
  • Transitional lumbosacral bodies occur in up to 25% of normal patients.
  • Sacralization ranges from expanded L5 transverse processes articulating with the sacrum to incorporation of L5 into the sacrum.
  • Lumbarization describes elevation of S1 above the sacral fusion mass with a lumbar vertebral body shape.
  • Sacralization and lumbarization may look similar locally; correct level nomenclature requires evaluation of the entire spinal axis.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Use regional morphology to orient the vertebral level, use ligament course and attachment to map the spinal support structures, and always treat lumbosacral transitional anatomy as a normal variant that can create level-naming errors unless the entire spinal axis is evaluated.

column9: Do not rely on the local lumbosacral appearance alone when transitional anatomy is present; whole-spine enumeration is needed to prevent wrong-level labeling.
column13: What is the key level-naming trap with sacralization and lumbarization?
column14: Sacralization and lumbarization may look similar in appearance. The safe move is to evaluate the entire spinal axis to define the anatomy and assign correct level nomenclature.

column9: This is a compact morphology-first regional identification framework derived from the article's normal anatomic descriptors.
column15: Which morphology anchors should be used to separate cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal vertebral regions?
column16: Cervical: transverse foramina, triangular central canal, uncinate processes, and small thin bodies. Thoracic: heart-shaped bodies, round canal, caudodorsal spinous processes, and rib costal facets. Lumbar: large thick bodies with robust pedicles and laminae. Sacrum: triangular fusion of 5 segments with alae and sacral foramina. Coccyx: fusion of 3-5 small segments with a caudally directed apex.
